name: Ribota Valley Villages Loop
name_original: Molino de Malanquilla - Berdejo - Torrijo de la Cañada — circular desde Villarroya de la Sierra
description: A difficult 32.0-mile touring cycling route from Villarroya de la Sierra, featuring rugged terrain and historic landmarks.
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Villarroya De La Sierra traverse a landscape characterized by a slightly sloping area leading to the Ribota river, with reddish hues defining the surroundings. The region, nestled at the foot of the Sierra de la Virgen, offers varied terrain suitable for different cycling experiences, from secondary roads to forest tracks. Historical fortified structures and ancient village settings provide additional points of interest along the routes. This area in Aragon presents a diverse environment for touring cyclists to explore. Best touring cycling routes around Villarroya De La Sierra The most popular touring cycling route is Ribota Valley Villages Loop, a 32.0 miles (51.6 km) trail that takes 3 hours 54 minutes to complete. This difficult route explores the scenic Ribota river valley and connects several local villages. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Pope Luna and Mesones de Isuela Castles Loop, a difficult 17.2 miles (27.6 km) path. This route features historical castles and varied terrain, offering a blend of cultural and natural exploration. Local touring cyclists also love the View of Calcena – Calcena loop from Illueca, a 39.8 miles (64.1 km) trail leading through varied landscapes with views towards Calcena, often completed in about 4 hours 9 minutes. Touring cycling around Villarroya De La Sierra is defined by varied terrain, historical fortified structures, and an extensive network of secondary roads and forest tracks. The region offers options for different ability levels, though many routes present a significant challenge. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 40 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Villarroya De La Sierra's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Villarroya De La Sierra?
A: There are 14 touring cycling routes around Villarroya De La Sierra. These routes offer diverse experiences, primarily catering to cyclists looking for a challenge.

Q: What is the general difficulty level of touring cycling routes in this area?
A: The touring cycling routes around Villarroya De La Sierra are generally considered &lt;strong&gt;difficult&lt;/strong&gt;. They often feature significant elevation gains and varied terrain, making them suitable for experienced cyclists seeking a challenging ride.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in Villarroya De La Sierra?
A: Yes, many of the touring cycling routes in the area are circular. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e993359229/ribota-valley-villages-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ribota Valley Villages Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e994096538/pope-luna-and-mesones-de-isuela-castles-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pope Luna and Mesones de Isuela Castles Loop&lt;/a&gt; are both excellent circular options, allowing you to start and finish at the same point.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes around Villarroya De La Sierra?
A: The terrain around Villarroya De La Sierra is quite varied, characterized by a slightly sloping area leading to the Ribota river, with distinctive reddish hues. You'll encounter a mix of secondary roads and forest tracks, often with challenging ascents and descents. The region is nestled at the foot of the Sierra de la Virgen, contributing to its diverse landscape.

Q: What historical sites or landmarks can I explore while cycling in the region?
A: The area is rich in history. You can explore ancient fortified structures like the 'Castillo de la Reina' and 'Castillo del Rey', along with well-preserved sections of the walled enclosure, including the 'Puerta Baja' and 'Puerta Vieja' with Mudéjar characteristics. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e994096538/pope-luna-and-mesones-de-isuela-castles-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pope Luna and Mesones de Isuela Castles Loop&lt;/a&gt; specifically highlights historical castles.

Q: Are there specific viewpoints or natural features to look out for on the routes?
A: Yes, the region offers several natural features and viewpoints. You can find impressive rock formations like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5303856" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Far West Bilbilitano Rock Formations&lt;/a&gt; and enjoy panoramic views from the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5303848" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillejos Viewpoint (Sierra de Armantes)&lt;/a&gt;. Peaks such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5247466" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cruz de Armantes&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5286167" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico Cabrera (1,433 m) – Sierra de la Virgen&lt;/a&gt; also offer stunning vistas.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Villarroya De La Sierra?
A: While specific seasonal data isn't provided, the varied terrain and potential for challenging ascents suggest that spring and autumn would likely offer the most comfortable temperatures for touring cycling. Summers can be hot in Aragon, and winters may bring colder conditions, especially at higher elevations.

Q: Are there any family-friendly touring cycling routes in the area?
A: Given that all listed touring cycling routes are rated as 'difficult' with significant elevation gains, they may not be suitable for young children or beginners. The area is more geared towards experienced cyclists looking for a challenge. For family outings, you might need to seek out less strenuous secondary roads not specifically listed as dedicated touring routes.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the touring cycling trails around Villarroya De La Sierra?
A: While there are no specific restrictions mentioned for dogs on these touring cycling routes, it's always advisable to ensure your dog is fit enough for potentially long and difficult rides, especially on varied terrain and secondary roads. Always keep your dog under control and be mindful of local wildlife and other trail users.

Q: Where can I find parking facilities for my bike near the routes?
A: Specific parking locations for individual routes are not detailed in the guide. However, as Villarroya De La Sierra is a village, you can typically find parking in or near the village center, which would provide access to the start points of many routes. Always check local signage for any parking restrictions.

Q: Is public transport available to reach the cycling routes with a bicycle?
A: Information regarding public transport options that accommodate bicycles to Villarroya De La Sierra is not readily available. It is recommended to check with regional bus or train services in Aragon for their specific policies on transporting bicycles, as this can vary greatly.

Q: Are there any permits or local regulations I should be aware of for cycling in Villarroya De La Sierra?
A: There are no specific permits mentioned for general touring cycling on the routes around Villarroya De La Sierra. However, it's always good practice to respect private property, stay on marked paths, and be aware of any local regulations regarding protected natural areas or historical sites you might pass through.

Q: What is the longest touring cycling route in the area?
A: The longest touring cycling route listed is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31887832" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Calatayud – ⭐️ Puerta de Terrer loop from Illueca&lt;/a&gt;, which covers approximately 73.2 kilometers (45.5 miles) and typically takes around 4 hours and 40 minutes to complete. This route offers a significant challenge for endurance cyclists.
tips:
It is a beautiful windmill that stands out on the plain of the municipality of Malanquilla. You can visit inside if we book a guided tour where they explain all the history and how it works.

At night, it is also beautiful, since it is very well lit.

Located in the Manubles Valley we find another of the many charming towns with a lot of architecture and history in this area of Aragon. The imposing Castle stands out, of which the remains remain today, and the spectacular church of San Millán located on top of a rocky crag.

Torrijo de la Cañada is a town located in the Manubles river valley that will undoubtedly leave us speechless. In Torrijo de la Cañada there was an episode that left a mark in the history of Spain during the Celtiberian wars. We can also visit the Church of Nuestra Señora del Hortal, the Hermitage or the old bridge where we are. And on the hills that rise above the town, there are more than a hundred old wineries that give us an unusual and wonderful image.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this touring bicycle route?
faqs.answer: This challenging touring bicycle route is estimated to take approximately 3 hours and 54 minutes to complete. This duration is for the full 51.6-kilometer loop.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is categorized as a difficult touring bicycle ride. It requires a very good fitness level due to significant ascents and descents, with an elevation gain of 660 meters. Be prepared for potentially steep or unpaved sections where you might need to push your bike.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on the Molino de Malanquilla - Berdejo - Torrijo de la Cañada route?
faqs.answer: The terrain on this route is rugged, typical of the Iberian Cordillera. While specific details for this exact loop are not fully mapped, similar routes in the Aragon region often feature a mix of forest tracks and paved sections. Expect varied surfaces and challenging gradients.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the way?
faqs.answer: You'll encounter several notable highlights on this route. These include the historic Malanquilla Windmill, the impressive Castle and Church of San Millán de Berdejo, and the charming settlement of Torrijo de la Cañada, which offers panoramic views and lush pine forests.

faqs.question: Where can I park when starting this cycling route from Villarroya de la Sierra?
faqs.answer: While specific parking areas for this exact trailhead in Villarroya de la Sierra are not detailed, it's generally advisable to look for available street parking within the village or designated public parking areas. Arriving early can help secure a spot, especially during peak seasons.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route suitable for beginners or families?
faqs.answer: No, this route is not suitable for beginners or families. It is classified as a 'difficult' touring bicycle ride requiring a very good fitness level and experience with challenging terrain, including significant ascents and descents.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route is typically during spring (April-May) and autumn (September-October). These seasons offer more moderate temperatures, which are ideal for a demanding ride. Summer can be very hot in Aragon, and winter may bring cold temperatures and potential snow at higher elevations.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this touring bicycle route?
faqs.answer: While there are no explicit restrictions against dogs on this specific touring bicycle route, it's important to consider the challenging nature of the ride. If you bring a dog, ensure it is well-conditioned for long distances and varied terrain, and always keep it on a leash for safety and respect for local wildlife and other users.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay entrance fees to cycle this trail?
faqs.answer: No, there are no special permits or entrance fees required to access and cycle this route. It traverses public lands and roads in the Aragon region.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes, restaurants, or accommodation options along the route or near the start?
faqs.answer: Yes, you will find amenities in the settlements along or near the route. Torrijo de la Cañada and Berdejo, as well as Villarroya de la Sierra (your starting point), are likely to offer cafes, restaurants, and potentially accommodation options. It's always a good idea to check opening hours in advance, especially in smaller villages.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with any other official trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route overlap with several official trails. You will encounter parts of the Valle del Ribota, Camino de Clares a Malanquilla, Camino de Berdejo a Malanquilla, and the SL-Z3 Sendero Local de la Cueva de la Cabrera. Berdejo, among others.
